event: Early Neolithic communities settle in the foothills of northern Mesopotamia, beginning the transition to farming.
The Ubaid culture spreads across southern Mesopotamia, establishing irrigation-based villages and early temples.
The Uruk period sees the rise of the world's first cities in southern Mesopotamia.
Cuneiform writing develops at Uruk, marking one of humanity's earliest writing systems.
Sumerian city-states such as Ur, Uruk, Lagash, and Kish flourish during the Early Dynastic period.
Sargon of Akkad founds the Akkadian Empire, the world's first known multinational empire.
Ur-Nammu founds the Third Dynasty of Ur, ushering in a Sumerian renaissance and one of the earliest law codes.
The Amorite dynasty establishes Babylon as a rising power in central Mesopotamia.
Hammurabi becomes king of Babylon and later issues his famous law code.
The Hittites sack Babylon, ending the First Babylonian Dynasty and ushering in Kassite rule.
Assyria rises as a major power in northern Mesopotamia under kings like Ashur-uballit I.
The Neo-Assyrian Empire begins its expansion, becoming the dominant power of the ancient Near East.
Sennacherib makes Nineveh the magnificent capital of the Assyrian Empire.
Ashurbanipal reigns over Assyria and assembles the great library at Nineveh.
A coalition of Babylonians and Medes sacks Nineveh, destroying the Assyrian Empire.
Nebuchadnezzar II ascends the throne, leading the Neo-Babylonian Empire to its zenith.
Cyrus the Great of Persia conquers Babylon, incorporating Mesopotamia into the Achaemenid Empire.
Alexander the Great defeats the Persians at Gaugamela and takes Babylon, beginning Hellenistic rule.
Alexander the Great dies in Babylon, and his empire is divided among his generals.
The Parthian Empire conquers Mesopotamia, establishing Ctesiphon as a key capital.
The Sasanian Empire supplants the Parthians and rules Mesopotamia with Ctesiphon as its capital.
Arab Muslim armies defeat the Sasanians at the Battle of al-Qadisiyyah, bringing Islam to Mesopotamia.
The Abbasid caliph al-Mansur founds Baghdad, which becomes the capital of the Islamic world.
Caliph al-Ma'mun establishes the House of Wisdom in Baghdad, a center of the Islamic Golden Age.
Mongol forces under Hulagu Khan sack Baghdad, ending the Abbasid Caliphate.
Timur (Tamerlane) sacks Baghdad, devastating the city for a second time within a century.
The Safavid Persians under Shah Ismail capture Baghdad.
The Ottoman Empire under Suleiman the Magnificent conquers Baghdad, beginning centuries of Ottoman rule.
Safavid Persia briefly recaptures Baghdad amid Ottoman-Persian wars.
The Ottomans retake Baghdad, securing lasting control over the Mesopotamian provinces.
Ottoman reformer Midhat Pasha modernizes Baghdad province with new administration and infrastructure.
British forces invade Ottoman Mesopotamia during World War I, landing at Basra.
British troops capture Baghdad from the Ottomans.
The League of Nations grants Britain the Mandate for Mesopotamia, sparking a major nationalist revolt.
Faisal I is crowned king, establishing the Kingdom of Iraq under British mandate.
Oil is discovered in commercial quantities at Baba Gurgur near Kirkuk.
Iraq gains formal independence and joins the League of Nations as a sovereign state.
Britain intervenes militarily to suppress a pro-Axis coup led by Rashid Ali al-Gaylani.
A military coup overthrows and kills King Faisal II, abolishing the monarchy and proclaiming a republic.
The Ba'ath Party seizes power in a coup, later consolidating control over the state.
A Ba'athist coup brings the party to lasting power, with Saddam Hussein rising as a key figure.
Iraq nationalizes the Iraq Petroleum Company, taking control of its oil industry.
Saddam Hussein becomes president of Iraq and launches a sweeping purge of rivals.
Iraq invades Iran, beginning the eight-year Iran-Iraq War.
The Iran-Iraq War ends, and Iraqi forces carry out chemical attacks on Kurdish civilians at Halabja.
Iraq invades and annexes Kuwait, triggering international condemnation and sanctions.
A U.S.-led coalition expels Iraqi forces from Kuwait in the Gulf War, followed by Kurdish and Shia uprisings.
A U.S.-led invasion topples Saddam Hussein's regime, beginning a prolonged occupation and insurgency.
Iraqis approve a new constitution and hold democratic elections under the new political order.
Saddam Hussein is executed after conviction by an Iraqi tribunal, amid escalating sectarian violence.
U.S. forces complete their withdrawal from Iraq, formally ending the war.
The Islamic State seizes Mosul and large areas of Iraq, triggering a major military campaign to defeat it.
Iraqi forces recapture Mosul and declare victory over the Islamic State.
Mass anti-government protests erupt across Iraq over corruption, unemployment, and poor services.
